channel.title: Kyunolab Mystery Archive
channel.description: Legends, folklore, mysteries, and strange tales.

item.title: Kidney Theft Urban Legend: Bathtub Ice, Travel Fear, and the Organ Theft Rumor
item.description: The kidney theft urban legend usually describes a traveler who is drugged, wakes in a bathtub full of ice, and discovers that one or both kidneys have been removed. The story spread widely through fax, email, and word of mouth in the 1990s.

item.title: D.B. Cooper Hijacking: Ransom, Parachute, and the Unsolved Man in the Suit
item.description: D.B. Cooper is the name attached to the unknown man who hijacked Northwest Orient Flight 305 on November 24, 1971, received $200,000 in ransom, and parachuted from the aircraft into the night. His identity and fate remain unresolved.

item.title: Island of the Dolls: Xochimilco, Don Julian, and the Strange Place Legend
item.description: The Island of the Dolls is a strange place in the Xochimilco canals near Mexico City, known for trees and structures covered with old dolls. The common legend links the dolls to Don Julian Santana and the spirit of a drowned girl.

item.title: Tunguska Event: Siberian Explosion, Flattened Forest, and the Natural Mystery That Changed the Sky
item.description: The Tunguska Event was a massive explosion over Siberia on June 30, 1908. Modern scientific summaries generally interpret it as an asteroid or bolide airburst that flattened forest over a huge area without leaving a normal crater.

item.title: Narcissus and Echo: Reflection, Voice, and the Greek Myth of Unreachable Love
item.description: Narcissus and Echo is a Greek myth about two forms of unreachable love. Echo can only repeat the words of others, while Narcissus becomes trapped by his own reflection and cannot reach the image he desires.

item.title: Spear of Destiny: Holy Lance, Relic Tradition, and the Object That Claims History
item.description: The Spear of Destiny is a legendary name often attached to the Holy Lance, the spear associated with the piercing of Christ in Christian tradition. Several relic traditions exist, and later occult stories added claims of world-shaping power.

item.title: Cockatrice Folklore: Deadly Glance, Serpent-Rooster Form, and Medieval Fear
item.description: The cockatrice is a medieval fabulous creature often imagined as part rooster and part serpent. It was believed to kill through a deadly look, breath, touch, or poison, and it is closely related to basilisk traditions.

item.title: Flatwoods Monster: Fireball, Witnesses, and the West Virginia Legend
item.description: The Flatwoods Monster is a West Virginia legend that began near Flatwoods on September 12, 1952, after witnesses saw a fireball and then reported a tall, strange figure near a hilltop. The story became part of regional UFO and monster folklore.

item.title: Mu Lost Continent: James Churchward, Occult Geography, and the Pacific Atlantis
item.description: Mu is a lost-continent legend popularized by James Churchward in the early 20th century. He claimed that a vanished Pacific civilization was the motherland of humanity, but the story belongs to occult geography and pseudoarchaeology rather than accepted history.

item.title: Fountain of Youth: Legendary Water, Ponce de Leon, and the Place People Keep Seeking
item.description: The Fountain of Youth is a long-lived legend about water that restores youth or preserves life. Its most famous modern association is Juan Ponce de Leon and Florida, but historians are cautious about treating that connection as fact.

item.title: Friday the 13th Origin: Superstition, Metafolklore, and the Making of an Unlucky Date
item.description: Friday the 13th is a modern superstition that combines two older streams of belief: unlucky Fridays and the uneasy number thirteen. The combined date is much easier to document in the 19th and early 20th centuries than in the Middle Ages.

item.title: Cicada 3301: Internet Puzzle, Cryptography, and the Mystery of an Unknown Group
item.description: Cicada 3301 is the name given to a series of anonymous internet puzzles that appeared in the early 2010s. The puzzles used cryptography, steganography, literature, music, Tor sites, and physical coordinates, turning an online challenge into a global hunt.

item.title: Pied Piper of Hamelin: Children, Rats, and the Folklore of a Vanishing
item.description: The Pied Piper of Hamelin is a German legend about a piper who removes rats from Hamelin and then leads away the town children after the citizens refuse to pay him. The familiar rat-catcher version is later than the oldest child-loss tradition, but the two parts now shape the story together.

item.title: Medusa and Perseus: The Gorgon, the Mirrored Shield, and the Power of the Severed Head
item.description: A source-aware Kyunolab article tracing Medusa, Perseus, the Gorgon sisters, the mirrored shield, and the strange afterlife of the severed head.

item.title: Arachne Myth: Athena, Weaving, and the Origin of the Spider
item.description: A source-aware Kyunolab record tracing Arachne, Athena, the weaving contest, and the spider transformation.

item.title: Pandora's Jar: The Greek Myth Behind Pandora's Box
item.description: A remade archive reading of Pandora's jar, the later box tradition, and the question of why hope was left inside.

item.title: Ra’s Solar Boat: Egyptian Myth, Night Journey, and the Daily Return of the Sun
item.description: A unified-policy Kyunolab reading of Ra's Solar Boat, based on existing external tradition rather than newly invented archive material.

item.title: Maui Fishing Up the Islands: Polynesian Myth, Trickster Hero, and Land From the Sea
item.description: A unified-policy Kyunolab reading of Maui Fishing Up the Islands, based on existing external tradition rather than newly invented archive material.

item.title: Anansi the Spider: Trickster Stories, Wisdom, and the Web of Tales
item.description: A unified-policy Kyunolab reading of Anansi the Spider, based on existing external tradition rather than newly invented archive material.

item.title: Amaterasu Cave Myth: Hidden Sun, Ritual, and the Return of Light
item.description: A unified-policy Kyunolab reading of Amaterasu Cave Myth, based on existing external tradition rather than newly invented archive material.
